main_summary.summary: Alfred Moldovan, a New York physician born in 1921 to Hungarian Jewish immigrants in Manhattan's Yorkville and raised in the Bronx, recounts his path from City College and WWII service as a 15th Air Force radar officer in Foggia, Italy, through Chicago Medical School to a practice in East Harlem. Moldovan describes co-founding the Medical Committee for Human Rights (MCHR) after a 1964 call from civil rights organizers, serving as the group's secretary-treasurer and raising approximately half a million dollars in two years. He recounts a 1964 deployment to Meridian, Mississippi during Freedom Summer, where he helped arrange Dr. David Spain's independent autopsy after the bodies of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner were recovered. In Selma in spring 1965, he was at the Edmund Pettus Bridge during the first march, crossed it alone after being threatened by the sheriff to reach injured marchers, and delivered the medical instructions to demonstrators at Brown Chapel the night before the second march. Moldovan describes being seated next to Dr. King in a car during travel as the designated physician prepared to take a bullet, and discusses internal MCHR splits over Vietnam and over whether to fund Southern clinics versus continue movement-support work.
key_themes: Medical Committee for Human Rights founding and fundraising
Freedom Summer medical presence in Mississippi
Selma voting rights campaign and Edmund Pettus Bridge
Jewish immigrant identity and Bronx upbringing
Physician activism and the limits of medical practice in the South
main_summary.historical_significance:
Moldovan's testimony documents the operational structure of the Medical Committee for Human Rights from a co-founder's perspective: the call from civil rights organizers, the founding meeting in Mike Holloman's office, the secretary-treasurer fundraising role, and the rotation system that sent physicians to Mississippi and Alabama on numbered tours. His firsthand account of the independent autopsy arranged for the bodies of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner — naming Dr. David Spain of Brooklyn as the physician brought down to perform it — provides a specific source for that episode of Freedom Summer.

The Selma sections supply a participant-observer record of the first Pettus Bridge crossing, the Brown Chapel address before the second march, and the role assigned to MCHR physicians as a protective body around Dr. King during travel between events. Moldovan's discussion of MCHR internal politics — the Vietnam-funding split, his disagreement with the clinic-building faction associated with Jack Geiger, and his decision to leave active involvement after 1966 — clarifies the divisions that fractured the organization in its second phase.

chapters.title: Hungarian Yorkville and the Bronx
chapters.summary: Moldovan describes being born in 1921 in Hungarian Yorkville on East 74th Street in Manhattan and moving to Brook Avenue in the East Bronx. He recounts his father's emigration in 1914 from the Carpathian Mountains, his work as a bread baker for the Pechter Baking Company, and his mother's seamstress trade and multilingual education. Moldovan speaks of growing up in Yiddish, an extended-family household, and the death of his younger brother from diphtheria.
related_events: Eastern European Jewish immigration to New York

chapters.title: City College and Pre-Medicine
chapters.summary: Moldovan describes attending City College — "the Harvard of the working class" — and graduating in 1942 while simultaneously working at the Oppenheim-Collins Building on 34th Street as an assistant resident buyer. He explains the Jewish-quota constraints on medical-school admission and his fallback plan to enter via a Ph.D. in bacteriology.
related_events: Pre-WWII Jewish-quota era in U.S. medical schools

chapters.title: Air Force Radar Officer in Foggia
chapters.summary: After Pearl Harbor, Moldovan enlisted, worked at the Belmar Signal Laboratories as a radio mechanic, then was called to active duty as an aviation cadet at Boca Raton, commissioned at Yale, and trained as an electronic specialist at Harvard and MIT. He served overseas with the 15th Air Force 455th Bomb Group at Foggia, Italy, supervising two hundred mechanics tending radar equipment on B-24s and later B-17s. He returned home when his mother fell ill rather than transferring to the Pacific.

chapters.title: Chicago Medical School and Political Awakening
chapters.summary: Moldovan describes attending Chicago Medical School — a former Flexner-Report B-rated school that dean John J. Scheinen elevated to Grade A status — graduating in 1950, and using GI Bill support alongside paid work. He recounts becoming politically active in medical school: helping found the Association of Interns and Medical Students (AIMS) and the American Veterans Committee (AVC), recruiting minority and women medical students, and integrating South Chicago beaches before both AIMS and AVC were red-baited out of existence during the Cold War.

chapters.title: East Harlem Practice and Physicians Forum
chapters.summary: After internship at Montefiore Hospital in the Bronx, pathology residency at Maimonides, and internal medicine at Kings County, Moldovan opened a practice in East Harlem to serve working-class people "like my father." He describes joining the Physicians Forum, becoming active on the County Medical Society's executive committee, and chairing its medical economics committee — including fighting against the dysfunctional initial design of Medicaid.

chapters.title: Founding MCHR and Meridian 1964
chapters.summary: Moldovan recounts the call from civil rights activists for medical support, the founding meeting in Mike Holloman's office, his election as secretary-treasurer of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, and his role in raising approximately half a million dollars in two years. He describes his August 1964 deployment to Meridian, Mississippi during Freedom Summer — "lending a presence" rather than practicing medicine, which was prohibited — and arranging for Dr. David Spain of Brooklyn to come down and perform an independent autopsy on Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner after the official Meridian pathologist's report was deemed inadequate.
related_events: Murder of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner

chapters.title: Selma and the Pettus Bridge
chapters.summary: Moldovan describes being sent to Selma with Virginia (Ginny) Wells, wife of Aaron Wells, as MCHR's next chairman after Holloman. He recounts arriving at the Edmund Pettus Bridge during the first march, being threatened by the sheriff that his head would be "blown off" if he crossed, crossing alone to drag tear-gassed people from houses, and seeing John Lewis with a concussion. The night before the second march, he addressed demonstrators at Brown Chapel with medical instructions — including the line about making sure someone witnesses if you are knocked unconscious. He describes Dr. King's deal with Washington to halt at the bridge during the second march, and his assignment seated beside King during travel to take a bullet if one came through the window.

chapters.title: MCHR Splits and Later Reflections
chapters.summary: Moldovan recounts a Washington meeting he organized that brought the heads of the NAACP, CORE, SCLC, and other major civil rights organizations onto one stage — describing it as unprecedented. He explains his opposition to MCHR taking up Vietnam as an organizational position because donors funding the committee included war supporters, and his disagreement with the faction associated with Jack Geiger that wanted to build Southern medical clinics rather than continue movement-support fundraising. He describes his withdrawal from active MCHR involvement after 1966, a later medical presence at Columbia University during the 1968 student occupation, a trip to Israel during the 1991 SCUD missile attacks, and his assessment that the U.S. health-care system remains one of the worst in the developed world.

loc_video.caption: Alfred Moldovan oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in New York, New York, 2011 July 19
